content.text: A model card or agent system card authored once and never updated is worse than no card at all: it describes a system that no longer exists, potentially misleading users, compliance teams, and regulators about what the agent actually does. Maintenance cadence — the discipline of knowing when, how, and by whom cards are reviewed and updated — is what separates governance documentation that works from documentation that merely exists.

attrs.alt: Model Card Maintenance Cadence: trigger-based and scheduled review types, five-step review process, and decision outcomes for minor vs major version updates

content.text: What it is

Model card maintenance cadence refers to the defined schedule and trigger conditions that govern when model cards and agent system cards are reviewed, updated, and republished. It encompasses both scheduled reviews (conducted on a calendar basis regardless of system changes) and event-triggered reviews (conducted when specific changes to the AI system occur). Together, these two maintenance modes ensure that cards remain accurate over the life of the system, through model updates, capability expansions, tool changes, system prompt revisions, and regulatory evolution.

Card maintenance is not just about keeping documentation current — it is about maintaining the integrity of the governance artefacts that compliance, legal, and audit functions rely on. When an audit committee member reads a model card or a regulator reviews it during an examination, they are making decisions based on its accuracy. An organisation that cannot demonstrate that its cards are current and have been reviewed on a documented schedule has a documentation governance problem that regulators treat as a proxy for inadequate AI programme governance more broadly.
Why it matters

Agentic AI systems change faster than traditional software: model providers release new versions frequently, tool integrations are added and modified as agent capabilities expand, and system prompts are iterated on based on production learnings. Each of these changes can materially alter the system's behaviour, risk profile, and applicable regulatory obligations. A card that accurately described the system at deployment can become significantly misleading within a few weeks if not actively maintained.

From a legal standpoint, an outdated card that misrepresents the agent's current capabilities is a more serious problem than no card, because it constitutes an affirmative misrepresentation rather than a documentation gap. If an incident occurs and the investigation reveals that the system card did not reflect the system at the time of the incident — for example, it described a model with read-only tool access when the system had actually gained write access in an update three months earlier — the organisation faces questions about why an inaccurate document was in circulation.
Benefits
Documentation integrity
— A maintained card accurately reflects the current system, making it a reliable reference for all stakeholders rather than a historical artefact.
Change-triggered compliance review
— Requiring a card review when the system changes forces compliance assessment of each change, not just at initial deployment. This catches changes that inadvertently cross regulatory thresholds.
Regulatory confidence
— A card with a current review date, a version history, and a documented review process demonstrates active governance to regulators and auditors.
Stakeholder trust
— Users and customers who can verify that a system card was reviewed recently and reflects the current system have more basis for trust than those relying on an undated document.
Organisational learning
— The periodic review process surfaces changes in the system and the regulatory environment that inform programme improvements beyond just the card update.
Trade-offs and limitations
Overhead proportional to change rate
— Agents that update frequently generate frequent review obligations. For teams iterating rapidly, the maintenance overhead can become a significant burden relative to the development velocity.
Change detection gaps

— Not all system changes are visible in the CI/CD pipeline; third-party model provider updates, API behavioural changes, and emerging failure modes discovered in production may not automatically trigger a review.
Materiality judgment

— Not every system change warrants a full card review; teams must exercise judgment about which changes are material enough to trigger an update. This judgment introduces inconsistency across teams without clear criteria.
Orphaned cards

— When a system is deprecated or significantly restructured, its associated cards may be left in the documentation system without being clearly marked as superseded, creating confusion about which documentation applies to what.
How it works

Card maintenance operates on two parallel tracks: scheduled reviews and trigger-based reviews. Scheduled reviews are calendar-driven check-ins at a cadence determined by the agent's risk tier: quarterly for high-risk agents (those classified as high-risk under internal policy or applicable regulations), semi-annually for medium-risk agents, and annually for low-risk and utility agents. Scheduled reviews are pre-booked in the card owner's calendar as standing obligations and must be completed and documented before the scheduled date.

Trigger-based reviews are initiated immediately when specific system changes occur. The defined trigger events include: any update to the base model version (including updates provided by the model provider without action by the deployment team); any addition, removal, or permission change for a tool in the agent's configuration; any material revision to the system prompt (defined as a revision that changes the agent's core task scope, prohibited actions, or response format); any change to data sources the agent queries; and any published update to applicable regulatory guidance that might affect the card's compliance mapping. Trigger events are detected through integration with the deployment pipeline and through a regulatory monitoring subscription.

Each review follows a five-step process: diff check (comparing the current system against the last card version to identify all changes); section-by-section accuracy review (verifying that each card section accurately describes the current system); compliance check (verifying that the card's regulatory mapping remains accurate against current requirements); sign-off by the card owner; and publication of the updated card with a new version tag, review date, and stakeholder notification.

Review outcomes fall into two categories: no material change (the system is substantially the same as described; the card's review date is updated and the card is reaffirmed; minor version incremented) or material change (one or more card sections require update; sections are revised, the card owner signs off, and a notification is sent to all stakeholders relying on the card; major version incremented).
Architecture

The maintenance cadence is implemented as a workflow integrated with three systems: the deployment pipeline (which generates trigger events when model versions, tool configurations, or system prompts change); the regulatory monitoring service (which generates trigger events when relevant regulatory guidance is updated); and the card management system (which owns the review workflow, versioning, owner assignment, and notification dispatch). The card management system maintains a review log for each card showing every review date, the reviewer identity, the outcome, and the card version issued.
When to use it

Formal maintenance cadence processes are required for any agent operating in a regulated domain or subject to external disclosure obligations. For internal utility agents in low-risk contexts, a lighter-touch approach — annual review with trigger-based check-in notifications — may be sufficient. For high-risk agents that are subject to regulatory examination or that the organisation has publicly committed to transparency reporting on, a rigorously enforced quarterly review cadence with documented outcomes is appropriate.
How to implement
Assign a named card owner to every card at creation time. Ownership is a required field in the card template; cards without owners are not accepted into the card management system.

Set the review cadence for each card based on the agent's risk tier: quarterly for high-risk, semi-annual for medium-risk, annual for low-risk. Pre-book reviews in the card owner's calendar at card creation.

Define the list of trigger events that initiate an out-of-cycle review and implement automated detection: deployment pipeline hooks for model version and tool configuration changes; regulatory monitoring subscriptions for relevant framework updates.

Author a review checklist for each card template that guides the reviewer through each section, prompting them to verify accuracy against the current system state rather than just re-reading the existing text.

Implement semantic versioning for cards: patch versions (x.y.Z) for minor corrections; minor versions (x.Y.0) for no-change reviews that update the review date; major versions (X.0.0) for material content updates.

Build a stale card detection report that identifies cards overdue for review (past scheduled review date) and cards that have not been reviewed following a detected trigger event. Route this report to the AI governance function weekly.
Include card currency status in the periodic compliance attestation: attest that all cards in scope were reviewed on schedule and that no trigger events remain unreviewed.
Examples

# Card maintenance tracking system
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import List, Optional
from enum import Enum

class RiskTier(Enum):
    LOW = 'low'
    MEDIUM = 'medium'
    HIGH = 'high'

REVIEW_CADENCE_DAYS = {
    RiskTier.LOW: 365,
    RiskTier.MEDIUM: 180,
    RiskTier.HIGH: 90,
}

TRIGGER_EVENTS = [
    'model_version_update',
    'tool_added',
    'tool_removed',
    'tool_permission_changed',
    'system_prompt_material_revision',
    'data_source_changed',
    'regulatory_guidance_updated',
    'significant_incident',
]

@dataclass
class CardReviewRecord:
    review_date: date
    reviewer: str
    trigger: str  # 'scheduled' or trigger event name
    outcome: str  # 'no_material_change' | 'material_change'
    version_issued: str
    notes: str = ''

@dataclass
class ModelCard:
    card_id: str
    agent_id: str
    owner: str
    risk_tier: RiskTier
    current_version: str
    last_review_date: date
    review_history: List[CardReviewRecord] = field(default_factory=list)
    pending_triggers: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def next_scheduled_review(self) -> date:
        cadence = REVIEW_CADENCE_DAYS[self.risk_tier]
        return self.last_review_date + timedelta(days=cadence)

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        return date.today() > self.next_scheduled_review()

    def has_unreviewed_triggers(self) -> bool:
        return len(self.pending_triggers) > 0

    def status_summary(self) -> dict:
        return {
            'card_id': self.card_id,
            'current_version': self.current_version,
            'last_review': self.last_review_date,
            'next_scheduled': self.next_scheduled_review(),
            'days_until_due': (self.next_scheduled_review() - date.today()).days,
            'overdue': self.is_overdue(),
            'pending_triggers': self.pending_triggers,
            'action_required': self.is_overdue() or self.has_unreviewed_triggers(),
        }

    def record_review(self, reviewer: str, trigger: str, outcome: str, new_version: str, notes: str = '') -> None:
        record = CardReviewRecord(
            review_date=date.today(),
            reviewer=reviewer,
            trigger=trigger,
            outcome=outcome,
            version_issued=new_version,
            notes=notes,
        )
        self.review_history.append(record)
        self.last_review_date = date.today()
        self.current_version = new_version
        if trigger in self.pending_triggers:
            self.pending_triggers.remove(trigger)

# Usage
card = ModelCard(
    card_id='CARD-CS-AGENT-001',
    agent_id='customer-service-agent-v3',
    owner='ai-team@company.com',
    risk_tier=RiskTier.HIGH,
    current_version='3.0.0',
    last_review_date=date(2025, 1, 15),
)

# Simulate model update trigger
card.pending_triggers.append('model_version_update')

print(card.status_summary())
# {'card_id': 'CARD-CS-AGENT-001', 'action_required': True, 'pending_triggers': ['model_version_update'], ...}

# After review
card.record_review(
    reviewer='jane.smith@company.com',
    trigger='model_version_update',
    outcome='material_change',
    new_version='3.1.0',
    notes='Updated model details section for base model v2.1; no capability changes',
)

# card-maintenance-policy.yaml
# Organisation Model Card Maintenance Policy v1.2

risk_tier_cadence:
  high:
    scheduled_review_days: 90
    max_days_overdue_before_escalation: 7
    escalation_to: head-of-ai-governance@company.com
  medium:
    scheduled_review_days: 180
    max_days_overdue_before_escalation: 14
    escalation_to: ai-compliance@company.com
  low:
    scheduled_review_days: 365
    max_days_overdue_before_escalation: 30
    escalation_to: card-owner

trigger_events:
  immediate_review_required:     # review within 5 business days
    - model_version_major_bump
    - tool_with_write_access_added
    - tool_permission_escalated
    - significant_incident
    - regulatory_requirement_change
  review_at_next_cycle:           # flag for next scheduled review
    - model_version_minor_bump
    - non-material_system_prompt_revision
    - performance_metric_update

versioning:
  major: 'X.0.0 — material content change; re-notify all stakeholders'
  minor: 'x.Y.0 — scheduled review with no material change; update date'
  patch: 'x.y.Z — correction of factual error; note correction in changelog'

review_checklist_required_sections:
  - identity_and_version_current
  - intended_use_still_accurate
  - model_details_reflect_deployed_version
  - tool_access_complete_and_accurate
  - limitations_include_newly_discovered_failure_modes
  - performance_metrics_reflect_current_monitoring_data
  - oversight_mechanisms_still_functional
  - regulatory_classification_still_correct

deprecation_process:
  - mark_card_status_as_deprecated
  - add_deprecation_date_and_reason
  - link_to_superseding_card_if_exists
  - retain_in_archive_for_minimum_5_years
Real-world example

A large retail bank maintained agent system cards for 12 deployed AI agents across their retail banking division. When Anthropic released a new major version of the base model used by their customer service agent, the deployment team updated the model version in their configuration and redeployed — standard practice. The card maintenance policy defined a model version major bump as an immediate-review trigger. However, the card owner was on leave, and the trigger notification went unactioned for three weeks.

During their quarterly attestation cycle, the compliance team's card currency report identified the unreviewed trigger. The card owner's manager was automatically notified per the escalation policy, and the review was completed within two days. The review found that the new model version had an expanded capability in code interpretation that was not described in the existing card. The card was updated (major version bump, stakeholder notification) and the incident was documented in the attestation as a process finding. The compliance team used this instance to tighten the trigger notification process: trigger notifications now go to both the card owner and their designated alternate simultaneously, rather than to the card owner alone.
Common mistakes
Single card owner with no alternate
— If the card owner is on leave when a trigger event occurs, the review stalls. Every card should have a named alternate owner who receives trigger notifications and can initiate reviews.
No trigger detection automation
— Relying on card owners to manually notice relevant changes in the deployment pipeline or regulatory landscape is unreliable. Triggers must be automatically detected and routed.
Treating scheduled reviews as checkbox exercises

— A review that consists of re-reading the card and confirming 'looks fine' without comparing against current system state adds no value. Reviews must use the review checklist to systematically verify each section.
No deprecation process

— When an agent is retired or replaced, its card should be marked as deprecated and archived, not deleted. Deleted cards cannot be retrieved for incident investigations or regulatory inquiries about historical system configurations.
Using one cadence for all agents
— Applying a quarterly review requirement uniformly to all agents, regardless of risk tier, either over-burdens teams managing low-risk tools or under-protects high-risk systems that change rapidly.
Best practices
Integrate trigger detection with the deployment pipeline
— Deployment pipelines know when model versions and tool configurations change; use webhooks or CI/CD hooks to automatically create card review tasks when trigger events occur.
Require a comparison artefact in every review
— The review record should include a brief diff: what changed in the system since the last review. This forces a systematic comparison rather than a surface-level reaffirmation.
Attest card currency in the compliance cycle
— Include card review completion status in the periodic compliance attestation, creating formal accountability for card maintenance at the programme level.
Use semantic versioning consistently
— Consistent versioning allows consumers of the card (compliance, legal, audit) to immediately understand the significance of a card update without reading the change log.
Notify stakeholders on major version changes
— Users, legal counsel, and compliance teams who rely on the card need to know when it has changed materially. Stakeholder notification should be automated on major version publication.
Performance and scalability

The maintenance overhead of a card review programme scales with the number of cards and the aggregate change rate across all agents. For a portfolio of 20 agents with a mix of risk tiers, scheduled reviews account for roughly 40-60 reviews per year; trigger-based reviews add to this depending on deployment velocity. At this scale, a single compliance analyst can manage the review tracking and escalation process. Above 50 agents, automation becomes essential: automated trigger detection, review task generation, owner notification, and stale card reporting are needed to prevent review obligations from accumulating undetected.

Comparison with alternatives

Overhead
Automated trigger + scheduled cadence (this topic)
High — systematic coverage of both change and time triggers

Security and reliability

The card maintenance system must itself be reliable; a system that generates false-positive trigger notifications causes review fatigue that leads card owners to ignore legitimate triggers. Monitor the trigger detection system for false positive rates and tune alert thresholds accordingly. Equally important is ensuring that no trigger events are silently dropped — implement dead-letter queues or alert watchdogs on the trigger pipeline so that missed events are surfaced.

Card version history must be tamper-evident: audit logs of who updated a card and when, with immutable storage of each version, protect against retrospective alteration of cards to make them appear accurate at a time when they were not. This is particularly important for cards associated with agents that have been involved in incidents, where the card's content at the time of the incident may be legally significant.
Key takeaways
Card maintenance requires both scheduled cadence reviews (calendar-driven by risk tier) and trigger-based reviews (event-driven by system and regulatory changes).
Trigger events must be automatically detected through deployment pipeline integration and regulatory monitoring; manual detection is too unreliable in a fast-changing agentic environment.
Every card must have a named owner and a named alternate; trigger notifications to a single point of contact create a single point of failure for the review process.
Semantic versioning of cards makes the significance of updates immediately legible to stakeholders without requiring them to read the change log.
An outdated card that inaccurately describes the current system is legally more problematic than no card, because it constitutes affirmative misrepresentation rather than a documentation gap.
Card currency status should be included in periodic compliance attestations, creating formal accountability for documentation governance at the programme level.
Deprecated cards must be archived, not deleted; they may be needed for incident investigations or regulatory inquiries about historical system configurations.
